Name: cuda_kernel_optimization
Title: Optimizing CUDA Kernels for Deep Learning Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: Carefully optimizing CUDA kernels for memory-bandwidth utilization and tensor-core occupancy can lead to significant improvements in deep learning inference speed on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: While there have been efforts to optimize deep learning models for inference speed, most of these efforts focus on high-level model architecture changes or quantization techniques. This proposal focuses on low-level kernel optimization, which has been relatively underexplored. Recent advancements in CUDA and GPU architecture provide new opportunities for optimization.
Abstract: This proposal aims to investigate the potential for optimizing CUDA kernels to improve deep learning inference speed on commodity GPUs. By focusing on memory-bandwidth utilization and tensor-core occupancy, we hope to achieve significant speedups without requiring specialized hardware. Our approach will involve profiling and optimizing individual kernels, as well as exploring the use of empirical tuning and autotuning techniques to find optimal kernel configurations. We will evaluate our approach using a range of deep learning models and datasets, and report results in terms of wall-clock latency, tokens-per-second, and energy per token.
Experiments: We will conduct a series of experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of our kernel optimization approach. These will include: (1) profiling and optimizing individual kernels for a range of deep learning models, (2) exploring the use of empirical tuning and autotuning techniques to find optimal kernel configurations, (3) evaluating the impact of our optimizations on wall-clock latency, tokens-per-second, and energy per token, and (4) comparing our results to existing state-of-the-art optimization techniques. We will use a range of deep learning models and datasets, including but not limited to ResNet, BERT, and ImageNet.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk of this proposal is that the optimizations we develop may not generalize well to different models or datasets. Additionally, the use of empirical tuning and autotuning techniques may require significant computational resources and may not always converge to optimal solutions. To mitigate these risks, we will carefully evaluate our approach using a range of models and datasets, and will explore the use of techniques such as kernel fusion and loop unrolling to reduce the complexity of our optimizations.
